meta.source: NIST SP 800-171 DoD Assessment Methodology v1.2.1 (2020-06-24), Annex A
meta.note: Verify against the official document before customer use.

controls.requirement: Limit system access to authorized users, processes acting on behalf of authorized users, and devices (including other systems).

guidance.plain: Every account on your systems belongs to a specific, approved person — no shared logins, no ghosts. If you can't say who 'shopfloor2' is, this control isn't met.
guidance.evidence: User list export from Microsoft 365 / Active Directory with a quarterly review sign-off; onboarding/offboarding checklist.
guidance.quick_win: Export your user list today and disable every account you can't match to a current employee.

controls.requirement: Limit system access to the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute.

guidance.plain: Having an account isn't enough — each person can only perform the transactions and functions their job needs. Roles and permissions, not everyone-an-admin. A shop-floor user shouldn't be able to change security settings or financial records.
guidance.evidence: A role-to-function matrix mapping job roles to permitted system actions; screenshots of group/role membership (M365/AD) showing least-function access; a periodic access review with sign-off.
guidance.quick_win: List your system/app roles and confirm no standard user has admin or cross-department functions they don't need — then remove the extras.

controls.requirement: Control the flow of CUI in accordance with approved authorizations.

guidance.plain: You control where CUI is allowed to move — which systems, people, and networks — and block the paths it shouldn't take (no CUI to personal email or a consumer cloud drive).
guidance.evidence: A data-flow diagram showing where CUI enters, is stored, and leaves; mail-flow / DLP rules restricting CUI destinations; a list of authorized flows.
guidance.quick_win: Write down the few places CUI actually lives and moves, then add one mail-flow or DLP rule blocking the most obvious wrong path (e.g., external auto-forwarding).

controls.requirement: Separate the duties of individuals to reduce the risk of malevolent activity without collusion.

guidance.plain: No single person should control a whole sensitive process end to end. Whoever requests a system change isn't the only one who approves it; whoever manages accounts isn't the sole reviewer of the logs — so one person can't act badly without a second noticing.
guidance.evidence: A duty-separation matrix or a documented example (request vs. approve vs. implement handled by different roles); evidence that admin and audit-review duties aren't the same single person.
guidance.quick_win: Pick your highest-risk system task (creating admin accounts, changing security configs) and require a different second person to approve or review it — and write down who does each part.

controls.requirement: Employ the principle of least privilege, including for specific security functions and privileged accounts.

guidance.plain: Everyone gets the minimum access to do their job and nothing extra. Admin rights only for people who truly need them. Fewer admins, tightly scoped — including for security functions.
guidance.evidence: A list of privileged/admin accounts each with a justification; evidence that local-admin rights are removed from standard user machines; a periodic privilege review.
guidance.quick_win: Pull your admin list today and cut it to the few who genuinely need it; remove local-admin from everyday user laptops.

controls.requirement: Use non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing nonsecurity functions.

guidance.plain: Even your admins use a normal (non-admin) account for everyday work — email, browsing, documents — and switch to their admin account only for admin tasks. That way a phished admin session doesn't hand over the keys.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that admins have separate standard and privileged accounts; a policy requiring non-privileged accounts for routine work.
guidance.quick_win: Give each admin a second, standard account for daily email/web, and reserve the admin account for admin work only.

controls.requirement: Prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions and capture the execution of such functions in audit logs.

guidance.plain: Regular users can't run admin-level functions, and whenever anyone does run a privileged function, it's recorded. Two parts: block it for non-admins, and log it when it happens.
guidance.evidence: Configuration showing standard users can't execute privileged functions; audit-log samples that capture privileged actions.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm standard users can't install software or change system settings, and that your audit logging captures admin actions (turn on unified/admin audit logging).

controls.requirement: Limit unsuccessful logon attempts.

guidance.plain: After a set number of wrong password tries, the account locks or slows down, so someone can't just keep guessing. A simple, standard setting.
guidance.evidence: An account-lockout policy screenshot (threshold and duration) from AD / Entra ID / Intune.
guidance.quick_win: Set an account-lockout threshold (commonly 5-10 attempts) in Entra ID/AD if it isn't already — it's a one-setting change.

controls.requirement: Provide privacy and security notices consistent with applicable CUI rules.

guidance.plain: Systems that handle CUI show a use/consent banner at login stating acceptable use, that activity may be monitored, and the applicable privacy/CUI-handling notice, consistent with applicable CUI rules.
guidance.evidence: A screenshot of the logon banner and the acceptable-use / privacy text it references.
guidance.quick_win: Add a login/consent banner (a Group Policy or Intune setting) with standard acceptable-use, monitoring, and privacy language.

controls.requirement: Use session lock with pattern-hiding displays to prevent access and viewing of data after a period of inactivity.

guidance.plain: Screens lock automatically after a few idle minutes and show a lock screen — not the work — so a walk-by can't read CUI on an unattended machine.
guidance.evidence: A policy/configuration setting the inactivity-lock timeout (e.g., 15 minutes) with a pattern-hiding lock screen, applied via GPO/Intune.
guidance.quick_win: Enforce an automatic screen lock (10-15 minutes) by policy across all machines.

controls.requirement: Terminate (automatically) a user session after a defined condition.

guidance.plain: Sessions end automatically under defined conditions — for example, sign-out after long inactivity or a maximum session length, especially for remote and web sessions. This is more than just locking the screen.
guidance.evidence: Configuration for session timeout/termination (VPN idle disconnect, web-app session timeout, Conditional Access sign-in frequency).
guidance.quick_win: Set an idle-disconnect on your VPN and a session timeout / sign-in frequency on your cloud apps.

controls.requirement: Monitor and control remote access sessions.

guidance.plain: All remote access into your environment goes through approved, monitored channels — you know who is connecting remotely and can see and manage those sessions. If you genuinely permit no remote access of any kind — no VPN, no remote desktop, and no reaching company systems/cloud from outside your offices — it may be marked N/A; but this is uncommon for shops using cloud email/apps, since reaching those from off-site is itself remote access. Document the basis in your SSP and the assessor must agree.
guidance.evidence: A list of approved remote-access methods (VPN, RMM); logs/monitoring of remote sessions; Conditional Access policies governing remote sign-in.
guidance.quick_win: Make sure remote access happens only through one approved, logged method (e.g., VPN with MFA) and turn on its connection logging.

controls.requirement: Employ cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions.

guidance.plain: Remote connections are encrypted in transit (e.g., VPN or TLS) so the session can't be read; for CUI the encryption should use FIPS-validated cryptography. Mark N/A only if you truly permit no remote access (see 3.1.12), you document it, and the assessor agrees.
guidance.evidence: VPN/TLS configuration showing encryption in use for remote paths, the protocol/cipher settings, and evidence the mechanism uses FIPS-validated cryptography (CMVP certificate / FIPS mode enabled).
guidance.quick_win: Confirm every remote-access path rides an encrypted tunnel (a modern VPN or TLS) and retire any plaintext remote protocol.

controls.short_title: Route remote access via control points
controls.requirement: Route remote access via managed access control points.

guidance.plain: Remote users come in through a small number of controlled gateways (a VPN concentrator or managed access point) — not scattered direct connections to individual machines.
guidance.evidence: A network diagram showing remote access funneled through defined gateways; firewall rules that enforce it.
guidance.quick_win: Ensure remote access terminates at one managed gateway, and block direct inbound remote connections to workstations.

controls.requirement: Authorize remote execution of privileged commands and remote access to security-relevant information.

guidance.plain: Doing admin-level work or reaching security-relevant information remotely requires explicit authorization — it isn't something any remote user can do by default.
guidance.evidence: A policy authorizing remote privileged actions; Conditional Access or privileged-access controls restricting remote admin; approval records.
guidance.quick_win: Restrict remote admin to specific authorized accounts and devices (Conditional Access), and document who is approved.

controls.requirement: Authorize wireless access prior to allowing such connections.

guidance.plain: Wireless connections are approved before they're allowed — you decide which devices and users can use Wi-Fi; it isn't open. If you permit no wireless at all, mark N/A and document it.
guidance.evidence: A wireless authorization policy; a controlled SSID with access control (802.1X or a pre-authorized device list); a separated guest network.
guidance.quick_win: Lock corporate Wi-Fi to authorized devices/users (WPA2/3-Enterprise or a device allowlist) and split off any guest network.

controls.requirement: Protect wireless access using authentication and encryption.

guidance.plain: Wi-Fi that touches your environment uses strong authentication and encryption (WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise) — not an open network or a weak shared password. Mark N/A only if no wireless is permitted.
guidance.evidence: Wireless configuration showing WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise (or equivalent) with authentication and encryption enabled, using FIPS-validated cryptographic modules where the wireless carries CUI.
guidance.quick_win: Move corporate Wi-Fi to WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise with per-user/device authentication; retire WEP/WPA-Personal for anything touching CUI.

controls.requirement: Control connection of mobile devices.

guidance.plain: Phones and tablets that connect to your systems are managed and controlled — you decide which devices connect and can enforce security on them. Mark N/A if no mobile devices are permitted to connect.
guidance.evidence: MDM/Intune enrollment showing managed mobile devices; Conditional Access requiring a compliant device.
guidance.quick_win: Require mobile devices to be enrolled and compliant (Intune) before they can reach email or company data.

controls.requirement: Encrypt CUI on mobile devices and mobile computing platforms.

guidance.plain: If CUI can land on a phone, tablet, or laptop, that device's storage is encrypted, so a lost or stolen device doesn't leak data.
guidance.evidence: Encryption enforcement using FIPS-validated encryption (e.g., BitLocker XTS-AES in FIPS mode, or MDM-enforced device encryption) with a compliance report.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on and enforce device encryption (BitLocker / mobile device encryption) via Intune and pull a compliance report.

controls.requirement: Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external systems.

guidance.plain: You verify and control how your systems connect to outside systems — partner networks, personal devices, third-party cloud — deciding which are allowed and what they can do. Note: this control can NOT be placed on a POA&M; it must be met at assessment.
guidance.evidence: A policy defining permitted external connections/services; firewall or Conditional Access limits enforcing it; an approved external-systems list.
guidance.quick_win: Write down which external systems and services are approved to connect, and block or restrict the rest — this one must be met, not deferred.

controls.short_title: Limit portable storage on external systems
controls.requirement: Limit use of portable storage devices on external systems.

guidance.plain: You limit using company USB and portable drives on outside or uncontrolled computers (and vice versa), where they could pick up malware or leak CUI.
guidance.evidence: A removable-media policy that addresses use on external systems; endpoint controls governing USB use.
guidance.quick_win: Add a policy line — and an endpoint control where possible — restricting company portable drives to managed machines only.

controls.requirement: Control CUI posted or processed on publicly accessible systems.

guidance.plain: Nothing containing CUI ends up on publicly accessible systems — your website, social media, or public file shares — and someone reviews and authorizes what goes public. Note: this control can NOT be placed on a POA&M; it must be met at assessment.
guidance.evidence: A policy and review process for public postings; evidence of who authorizes website/social content; confirmation no CUI sits on public shares.
guidance.quick_win: Assign one person to review anything before it goes on the public website or social media, and confirm no CUI is on public shares — must be met, not deferred.

controls.requirement: Ensure that managers, systems administrators, and users of organizational systems are made aware of the security risks associated with their activities and of the applicable policies, standards, and procedures related to the security of those systems.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Ensure that managers, systems administrators, and users of organizational systems are made aware of the security risks associated with their activities and of the applicable policies, standards, and procedures related to the security of those systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “security awareness for all users” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Ensure that personnel are trained to carry out their assigned information security-related duties and responsibilities.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Ensure that personnel are trained to carry out their assigned information security-related duties and responsibilities. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “role-based security training” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Provide security awareness training on recognizing and reporting potential indicators of insider threat.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Provide security awareness training on recognizing and reporting potential indicators of insider threat. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “insider-threat awareness” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Create and retain system audit logs and records to the extent needed to enable the monitoring, analysis, investigation, and reporting of unlawful or unauthorized system activity.

guidance.plain: Your systems keep records of who did what, when — and you keep those records long enough to investigate an incident months later. Default 30-day retention usually isn't enough.
guidance.evidence: Screenshot of audit log settings and retention period; a sample log pull for one user.
guidance.quick_win: Turn on Microsoft 365 unified audit logging and extend retention as far as your license allows.

controls.requirement: Ensure that the actions of individual system users can be uniquely traced to those users, so they can be held accountable for their actions.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Ensure that the actions of individual system users can be uniquely traced to those users, so they can be held accountable for their actions. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “trace actions to individual users” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Review and update logged events.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Review and update logged events. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “review & update logged events” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Alert in the event of an audit logging process failure.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Alert in the event of an audit logging process failure. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “alert on audit logging failure” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Correlate audit record review, analysis, and reporting processes for investigation and response to indications of unlawful, unauthorized, suspicious, or unusual activity.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Correlate audit record review, analysis, and reporting processes for investigation and response to indications of unlawful, unauthorized, suspicious, or unusual activity. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “correlate audit review & response” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Provide audit record reduction and report generation to support on-demand analysis and reporting.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Provide audit record reduction and report generation to support on-demand analysis and reporting. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “audit reduction & reporting” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Provide a system capability that compares and synchronizes internal system clocks with an authoritative source to generate time stamps for audit records.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Provide a system capability that compares and synchronizes internal system clocks with an authoritative source to generate time stamps for audit records. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “synchronized time stamps” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Protect audit information and audit logging tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Protect audit information and audit logging tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “protect audit info & tools” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.short_title: Limit audit management to few admins
controls.requirement: Limit management of audit logging functionality to a subset of privileged users.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Limit management of audit logging functionality to a subset of privileged users. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “limit audit management to few admins” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Establish and maintain baseline configurations and inventories of organizational systems (including hardware, software, firmware, and documentation) throughout the respective system development life cycles.

guidance.plain: A written inventory of every computer, server, and piece of software you own, plus a documented 'standard build' for how a new machine gets set up.
guidance.evidence: Asset inventory (export from Intune/RMM counts); a one-page standard configuration document.
guidance.quick_win: Export the device list from whatever management tool you have — that's the skeleton of your inventory.

controls.requirement: Establish and enforce security configuration settings for information technology products employed in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You don't run machines on factory defaults — there's a hardening checklist (disable what you don't use, enforce screen lock, etc.) and it's actually applied.
guidance.evidence: Group Policy / Intune configuration profiles; a hardening checklist referencing a benchmark like CIS.
guidance.quick_win: Adopt a published small-business hardening baseline instead of writing your own from scratch.

controls.requirement: Track, review, approve or disapprove, and log changes to organizational systems.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Track, review, approve or disapprove, and log changes to organizational systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “track & approve system changes” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Analyze the security impact of changes prior to implementation. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “analyze security impact of changes” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to organizational systems.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to organizational systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “access restrictions for changes” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring organizational systems to provide only essential capabilities.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Employ the principle of least functionality by configuring organizational systems to provide only essential capabilities. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “least functionality” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Restrict, disable, or prevent the use of nonessential programs, functions, ports, protocols, and services.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Restrict, disable, or prevent the use of nonessential programs, functions, ports, protocols, and services. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).

controls.requirement: Apply deny-by-exception (blacklisting) policy to prevent the use of unauthorized software or deny-all, permit-by-exception (whitelisting) policy to allow the execution of authorized software.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Apply deny-by-exception (blacklisting) policy to prevent the use of unauthorized software or deny-all, permit-by-exception (whitelisting) policy to allow the execution of authorized software. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “application allowlisting / denylisting” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Control and monitor user-installed software.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Control and monitor user-installed software. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “control user-installed software” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Identify system users, processes acting on behalf of users, and devices.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Identify system users, processes acting on behalf of users, and devices. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “identify users, processes, devices” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Authenticate (or verify) the identities of users, processes, or devices, as a prerequisite to allowing access to organizational systems.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Authenticate (or verify) the identities of users, processes, or devices, as a prerequisite to allowing access to organizational systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “authenticate before access” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Use multifactor authentication for local and network access to privileged accounts and for network access to non-privileged accounts.

guidance.plain: Multifactor authentication for everyone — not just email. Partial credit exists: MFA for admins and remote users only loses 3 points instead of 5. Full credit requires the general workforce too.
guidance.evidence: MFA status export from your identity provider showing per-user enforcement.
guidance.quick_win: Enforce MFA for admin and remote accounts this week (that alone moves you from −5 to −3), then roll out to everyone.

controls.requirement: Employ replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Employ replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “replay-resistant authentication” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “prevent identifier reuse” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Establish an operational incident-handling capability for organizational systems that includes preparation, detection, analysis, containment, recovery, and user response activities.

guidance.plain: When something bad happens, you have a written plan: who's in charge, who you call, how you contain it, how you recover. A plan that exists only in someone's head scores zero.
guidance.evidence: A written incident response plan with named roles; notes from at least one walkthrough or tabletop exercise.
guidance.quick_win: Draft a two-page IR plan from a free template and put real names and phone numbers in it.

controls.requirement: Track, document, and report incidents to designated officials and/or authorities both internal and external to the organization.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Track, document, and report incidents to designated officials and/or authorities both internal and external to the organization. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “track, document & report incidents” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Test the organizational incident response capability.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Test the organizational incident response capability. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “test incident response” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Perform maintenance on organizational systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.

guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “perform system maintenance” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Provide controls on the tools, techniques, mechanisms, and personnel used to conduct system maintenance.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Provide controls on the tools, techniques, mechanisms, and personnel used to conduct system maintenance. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “control maintenance tools & personnel” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Ensure equipment removed for off-site maintenance is sanitized of any CUI.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).

controls.requirement: Sanitize or destroy system media containing CUI before disposal or release for reuse.

guidance.plain: Before a computer, drive, or copier leaves your building — sold, recycled, returned off lease — the data on it is wiped or the drive is destroyed, and you keep a record.
guidance.evidence: Wipe/destruction log; certificates of destruction from your recycler.
guidance.quick_win: Start a simple disposal log today; ask your recycler for certificates going forward.

guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “mark media with cui markings” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.short_title: Encrypt CUI on media in transport
controls.requirement: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of CUI stored on digital media during transport unless otherwise protected by alternative physical safeguards.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of CUI stored on digital media during transport unless otherwise protected by alternative physical safeguards. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.

controls.requirement: Control the use of removable media on system components.

guidance.plain: USB sticks and external drives don't just get plugged in freely. You either block removable media by policy and technical control, or you control which specific devices are allowed.
guidance.evidence: Endpoint policy screenshot showing removable-media restrictions; written removable media policy.
guidance.quick_win: If you can't manage USB devices yet, block them outright — blocking fully satisfies this control.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “prohibit unowned portable storage” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “protect backups of cui” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “screen individuals before access” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “protect cui during personnel actions” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “limit physical access” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).

guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “physical access audit logs” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “manage physical access devices” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.short_title: Safeguard CUI at alternate work sites
controls.requirement: Enforce safeguarding measures for CUI at alternate work sites.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Enforce safeguarding measures for CUI at alternate work sites. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “safeguard cui at alternate work sites” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Periodically assess the risk to organizational operations (including mission, functions, image, or reputation), organizational assets, and individuals, resulting from the operation of organizational systems and the associated processing, storage, or transmission of CUI.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Periodically assess the risk to organizational operations (including mission, functions, image, or reputation), organizational assets, and individuals, resulting from the operation of organizational systems and the associated processing, storage, or transmission of CUI. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “periodic risk assessments” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Scan for vulnerabilities in organizational systems and applications periodically and when new vulnerabilities affecting those systems and applications are identified.

guidance.plain: Something regularly scans your systems for known security holes, and you look at the results. 'Our IT guy keeps an eye on things' is not a scan.
guidance.evidence: Recent vulnerability scan reports with dates; a note on scan frequency.
guidance.quick_win: Microsoft Defender's built-in vulnerability management or a free scanner tier gets you a first real report this week.

controls.requirement: Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Remediate vulnerabilities in accordance with risk assessments. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “remediate vulnerabilities” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Periodically assess the security controls in organizational systems to determine if the controls are effective in their application.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Periodically assess the security controls in organizational systems to determine if the controls are effective in their application. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “periodic security control assessment” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Develop and implement plans of action designed to correct deficiencies and reduce or eliminate vulnerabilities in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Develop and implement plans of action designed to correct deficiencies and reduce or eliminate vulnerabilities in organizational systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “plans of action (poa&m)” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Monitor security controls on an ongoing basis to ensure the continued effectiveness of the controls.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Monitor security controls on an ongoing basis to ensure the continued effectiveness of the controls. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “continuous control monitoring” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Develop, document, and periodically update system security plans that describe system boundaries, system environments of operation, how security requirements are implemented, and the relationships with or connections to other systems.

guidance.plain: The System Security Plan is the master document describing your environment and how each requirement is met. Under the DoD methodology, without an SSP there is no score at all — the assessment cannot be completed.
guidance.evidence: The SSP itself, with a revision date within the last year.
guidance.quick_win: Start from a free SSP template and fill in your real environment — an honest draft beats a blank page in every scenario.

controls.requirement: Monitor, control, and protect communications (i.e., information transmitted or received by organizational systems) at the external boundaries and key internal boundaries of organizational systems.

guidance.plain: You have a controlled, monitored edge to your network — a real managed firewall at the internet boundary (and at key internal boundaries, like around wherever CUI lives) that controls and inspects what crosses. Not just the ISP's box on default settings.
guidance.evidence: Firewall configuration/ruleset at the boundary; evidence of boundary monitoring/logging (firewall logs, IDS/IPS); a network diagram marking the external and key internal boundaries.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm a managed firewall sits at your internet edge with logging on, and identify the boundary around wherever CUI lives so you can protect it specifically.

controls.requirement: Employ architectural designs, software development techniques, and systems engineering principles that promote effective information security within organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Security is built into how your systems are designed and set up — you follow recognized secure-design and hardening principles (segmentation, least functionality, defense in depth) rather than bolting security on afterward. For a small shop this is largely documenting that you follow a known baseline/reference architecture.
guidance.evidence: A documented system/security architecture, or a reference to the secure baseline you follow (e.g., CIS Benchmarks, vendor security baselines); evidence that changes consider security impact.
guidance.quick_win: Write a short architecture note describing how your network is segmented and which hardening baseline you follow — that's the core artifact assessors look for here.

controls.requirement: Separate user functionality from system management functionality.

guidance.plain: The tools and interfaces used to administer systems are kept separate from the ones regular users use — admins manage systems through dedicated consoles and admin accounts, not from the same interface a standard user has.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that management interfaces are separate from user functionality (separate admin portals, a jump host, distinct admin accounts).
guidance.quick_win: Make sure system administration happens through dedicated admin consoles/accounts, not mixed into everyday user desktops.

controls.requirement: Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.

guidance.plain: Shared system resources (memory, temporary storage, scratch space) don't leak leftover data from one user or process to the next. For a small shop this is mostly a matter of running current, supported, patched operating systems, which clear resources between uses by default.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of supported/patched operating systems with default object-reuse protections in place; no shared scratch areas that expose residual data.
guidance.quick_win: Keep systems on current, supported, patched OS versions — object-reuse protection is built in; the real risk is unsupported or unpatched systems.

controls.requirement: Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks.

guidance.plain: Anything the public can reach — a web server or a public-facing service — sits on a separate network segment (a DMZ) walled off from your internal network, so a compromise of the public thing doesn't hand over the inside.
guidance.evidence: A network diagram showing public-facing components in a separate subnet/DMZ; firewall rules isolating that segment from the internal network.
guidance.quick_win: If you host anything publicly reachable, put it on a separate subnet/VLAN with firewall rules blocking it from initiating connections into your internal network. If you host nothing public, document that.

controls.short_title: Deny by default, allow by exception
controls.requirement: Deny network communications traffic by default and allow network communications traffic by exception (i.e., deny all, permit by exception).

guidance.plain: Your firewall blocks all traffic by default and only allows what you've explicitly permitted — both inbound and, importantly, outbound. The opposite (allow-all, block-a-few) is the common weak default this control exists to fix.
guidance.evidence: A firewall ruleset ending in an explicit default-deny; a documented list of explicitly-permitted flows; evidence that outbound traffic is also restricted, not just inbound.
guidance.quick_win: Add an explicit default-deny at the end of your firewall rules and review outbound rules — most shops allow all outbound, which this control expects you to tighten.

controls.requirement: Prevent remote devices from simultaneously establishing non-remote connections with organizational systems and communicating via some other connection to resources in external networks (i.e., split tunneling).

guidance.plain: When a device is connected to your network remotely (over VPN), it can't simultaneously bridge to the open internet in a way that routes around your protections — the VPN forces that device's traffic through your controls instead of out a second, uncontrolled path.
guidance.evidence: VPN configuration enforcing full-tunnel, or otherwise preventing split tunneling, for remote devices.
guidance.quick_win: Set your VPN to full-tunnel (or explicitly disable split tunneling) so remote sessions can't bridge your network to the open internet.

controls.requirement: Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure of CUI during transmission unless otherwise protected by alternative physical safeguards.

guidance.plain: CUI moving over networks is encrypted — TLS for web and email paths, VPN for site-to-site. If a file with CUI travels in the clear, this fails.
guidance.evidence: Configuration screenshots showing TLS enforcement / VPN settings for paths that carry CUI.
guidance.quick_win: Force TLS on your email connectors and require the VPN for any remote path that touches CUI.

controls.requirement: Terminate network connections associated with communications sessions at the end of the sessions or after a defined period of inactivity.

guidance.plain: Network sessions get torn down when they end or after a period of inactivity — connections don't linger open indefinitely (VPN idle timeout, server and firewall session timeouts).
guidance.evidence: Configuration showing network/session idle timeouts (VPN, servers, firewall session tables).
guidance.quick_win: Set idle timeouts on your VPN and key network services so idle connections drop automatically.

controls.requirement: Establish and manage cryptographic keys for cryptography employed in organizational systems.

guidance.plain: The keys and certificates behind your encryption are managed properly — generated, stored, rotated, and retired securely — not left in default states or shared insecurely.
guidance.evidence: A key/certificate management process, or evidence of managed keys (a certificate inventory with expiry dates, protected key storage, a rotation practice).
guidance.quick_win: Inventory your certificates and encryption keys with their expiry dates, and confirm private keys aren't sitting in shared or unprotected locations.

controls.requirement: Employ FIPS-validated cryptography when used to protect the confidentiality of CUI.

guidance.plain: Where encryption protects CUI, the cryptographic module must be FIPS-validated — using a strong algorithm isn't enough; the module needs a CMVP certificate. Partial credit: encryption in use but not FIPS-validated loses 3 points instead of 5.
guidance.evidence: List of encryption in use with CMVP certificate numbers (e.g., BitLocker in FIPS mode).
guidance.quick_win: Inventory where CUI is encrypted today, then check each product's FIPS validation status on the NIST CMVP database.

controls.requirement: Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and provide indication of devices in use to users present at the device.

guidance.plain: Cameras, microphones, and conferencing devices can't be turned on remotely without the people in the room knowing — there's a clear indication (a light or on-screen prompt) when they're active. This prevents covert activation.
guidance.evidence: Configuration/policy preventing remote activation of cameras/mics; evidence of in-use indicators; conferencing application settings.
guidance.quick_win: Confirm your collaboration apps show a clear in-use indicator and can't silently enable cameras/mics; set policies accordingly, or physically disable/cover devices where not needed.

controls.requirement: Control and monitor the use of mobile code.

guidance.plain: "Mobile code" — things like Office macros, JavaScript, ActiveX, and Java applets that run automatically — is controlled: you decide what's allowed to run and block risky types, especially from untrusted sources.
guidance.evidence: Policy and technical controls for mobile code (macro-blocking policies, browser/endpoint settings restricting active content).
guidance.quick_win: Block Office macros that come from the internet (a standard Microsoft 365 / Group Policy setting) — that's the highest-value mobile-code control for most shops.

controls.requirement: Control and monitor the use of Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technologies.

guidance.plain: If you use internet voice/phone systems (VoIP, or Teams/Zoom calling), their use is controlled and monitored — configured securely, not left wide open. If you use no VoIP at all, record that in your SSP.
guidance.evidence: VoIP configuration/policy showing secured, monitored use (segmentation, authentication), or SSP documentation that no VoIP is used.
guidance.quick_win: If you use VoIP, confirm it's on a controlled/segmented setup with authentication; if you don't, write a one-line SSP note that VoIP isn't used.

controls.requirement: Protect the authenticity of communications sessions.

guidance.plain: Communications sessions are protected so they can't be hijacked or tampered with mid-stream — which in practice comes from using authenticated, encrypted protocols (TLS, IPsec) rather than unauthenticated ones.
guidance.evidence: Evidence that sessions use authenticated/encrypted protocols (TLS everywhere, IPsec for site-to-site); configuration disabling weak or unauthenticated protocols.
guidance.quick_win: Enforce TLS on internal and external web/services and retire legacy unauthenticated protocols — that's what protects session authenticity in practice.

controls.requirement: Protect the confidentiality of CUI at rest.

guidance.plain: CUI sitting in storage — on drives, servers, databases, and backups — is protected so someone who gets the disk or file can't read the CUI. Encryption at rest is the usual mechanism (offline or physically-secured storage is an accepted alternative where encryption isn't feasible). When you use encryption to protect CUI, it must be FIPS-validated — that requirement comes from 3.13.11.
guidance.evidence: Evidence of encryption at rest wherever CUI is stored (BitLocker/volume encryption, database or file encryption), including backups; for the encryption used to protect CUI, the FIPS 140 CMVP validation certificate (certificate number) for the module. Where encryption isn't used, evidence of the alternative protection (e.g., secured offline storage).
guidance.quick_win: Turn on and enforce BitLocker/volume encryption everywhere CUI is stored, including backups, and record the module's CMVP certificate number so you can show the encryption is FIPS-validated (3.13.11).

controls.requirement: Identify, report, and correct system flaws in a timely manner.

guidance.plain: Security patches get applied on a schedule with deadlines — not 'when we get around to it.' You can show when a given patch landed.
guidance.evidence: Patch compliance report from your update tool; written patching cadence with deadlines.
guidance.quick_win: Set automatic updates with a defined maximum deferral and export your first compliance report.

controls.requirement: Provide protection from malicious code at designated locations within organizational systems.

guidance.plain: Anti-malware runs on every machine, it's centrally visible, and nobody can quietly turn it off.
guidance.evidence: Coverage report from your AV/EDR console showing all endpoints protected and current.
guidance.quick_win: Pull the coverage report and chase the stragglers — the gap machines are the whole risk.

controls.requirement: Monitor system security alerts and advisories and take action in response.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Monitor system security alerts and advisories and take action in response. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “monitor security alerts & act” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Update malicious code protection mechanisms when new releases are available.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Update malicious code protection mechanisms when new releases are available. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “update malware protection” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Perform periodic scans of organizational systems and real-time scans of files from external sources as files are downloaded, opened, or executed.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Perform periodic scans of organizational systems and real-time scans of files from external sources as files are downloaded, opened, or executed. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “periodic & real-time scans” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Monitor organizational systems, including inbound and outbound communications traffic, to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Monitor organizational systems, including inbound and outbound communications traffic, to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “monitor traffic for attacks” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.

controls.requirement: Identify unauthorized use of organizational systems.

guidance.plain: In plain terms: Identify unauthorized use of organizational systems. The test isn't whether it's written down — it's whether it actually happens and you can show it.
guidance.evidence: A policy or configuration establishing this requirement, plus a record that shows it operating (a screenshot, export, or log).
guidance.quick_win: Name who owns “identify unauthorized use” and capture one piece of evidence that it is operating today.
